question: Explain the Pigeonhole Principle in your own words and provide a simple, original example of its application.
expectedPoints: A clear definition: if more than k items are placed into k containers, then at least one container must contain more than one item.
A correct, original, non-textbook example (e.g., in any group of 13 people, at least two share a birth month).
Logical explanation connecting the example to the principle's statement.

question: Prove that in any set of five integers, there are at least two integers that have the same remainder when divided by 4.
expectedPoints: Identify the 'pigeonholes': the possible remainders when dividing by 4 are 0, 1, 2, and 3 (4 holes).
Apply the principle: 5 pigeons > 4 holes, so at least one hole must contain at least 2 pigeons.
Conclusion: Therefore, at least two integers must have the same remainder.

question: A company has 1000 employees. Each employee has a unique 4-digit employee ID. Prove that there must be at least two employees whose IDs sum to the exact same number. (Hint: Consider the possible range of sums.)
expectedPoints: Calculate the range of possible sums: The smallest ID is 0001 (sum=1), the largest is 9999 (sum=36).
Identify the number of possible distinct sums: There are 36 possible sum values (from 1 to 36).
Apply the Pigeonhole Principle: There are 1000 employees (pigeons) and only 36 possible sums (holes). 1000 / 36 ≈ 27.78.

Conclusion: Since 1000 > 36, by the principle, at least one sum value (hole) must be shared by at least 28 employees (ceil(1000/36)=28). Therefore, at least two employees must have IDs that sum to the same number.
